title: Dog Facial Twitching While Awake: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your dog's face twitching? Learn about facial tremors in awake dogs, why the urgency is usually low, and how video triage helps your veterinarian.
content_html:
What Is Facial Twitching?

Facial twitching, often referred to by veterinarians as focal seizures or localized muscle fasciculations, involves involuntary movements of the muscles in the muzzle, eyelids, or ears. Unlike full-body seizures, these episodes occur while your dog is fully awake and alert. It can be caused by a variety of factors ranging from harmless muscle fatigue to neurological irritation or even dental discomfort.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for facial twitching while awake is generally Low. If your dog is conscious, responsive, and acting normally otherwise, this is usually not an immediate life-threatening situation. However, you should monitor for any progression into full-body tremors or changes in behavior. While not an emergency, it does warrant a scheduled visit to your veterinarian to rule out underlying health issues.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Because these tremors are often intermittent, they may not occur while you are actually at the veterinary clinic. Capturing a video of the twitching is one of the most helpful things you can do for the triage process. A clear video allows the vet to see the frequency, rhythm, and specific muscle groups involved, which is essential for differentiating between a simple tic and a focal seizure.

faqs.question: Could my dog be having a small seizure?
faqs.answer: Yes, localized facial twitching can sometimes be a focal seizure. Unlike generalized seizures, your dog remains conscious, but the electrical activity in the brain is limited to a small area.

faqs.question: Can dental issues cause facial twitches?
faqs.answer: Yes, oral pain or nerve irritation from a bad tooth can cause the muscles in the cheek or jaw to spasm involuntarily.

faqs.question: What should I do during an episode?
faqs.answer: Stay calm and do not try to restrain your dog. Start recording a video on your phone and note how long the episode lasts to share with your veterinarian.
